pick.rationale: Georgia enters with a slight analytical edge at 31st in KenPom and 37th in Torvik, boasting an elite offense ranked 15th-18th nationally in adjusted offensive efficiency, while Saint Louis sits 41st-44th in the major metrics. The decisive factor is Saint Louis's injury situation: leading scorer Robbie Avila is dealing with plantar fasciitis that limited his minutes and kept him out of practice, casting significant doubt on his effectiveness for the NCAA tournament. While Saint Louis has the better defensive profile (56th in adjusted defense vs Georgia's 93rd) and a gaudy 28-5 record, Georgia tested itself nightly in the SEC with the 68th-toughest schedule compared to Saint Louis's 116th, and the Bulldogs' elite offense should exploit a Billikens team that stumbled to a 3-3 finish and lost to Dayton in the A-10 semifinals. The combination of Georgia's superior offensive firepower, SEC-tested pedigree, and Saint Louis's injury concerns gives the Bulldogs a narrow but meaningful advantage in this evenly-matched 8/9 game.

result.text: 2026 Atlantic 10 Tournament Previews: Saint Louis Billikens Close Menu News Tourney Central Blog Multimedia Photos Video Forums Teams Davidson Dayton Duquesne Fordham George Mason George Washington La Salle Loyola Rhode Island Richmond Saint Louis St. Bonaventure St. Joseph’s UMass VCU WBB Contributors Join Us Facebook X (Twitter) Instagram Facebook X (Twitter) Instagram News Tourney Central Blog Multimedia Photos Video Forums Teams Davidson Dayton Duquesne Fordham George Mason George Washington La Salle Loyola Rhode Island Richmond Saint Louis St. Bonaventure St. Joseph’s UMass VCU WBB Contributors Join Us You are at: Home » Blog » 2026 Atlantic 10 Tournament Previews: Saint Louis Billikens 2026 Atlantic 10 Tournament Previews: Saint Louis Billikens By Samuel Basel March 11, 2026 No Comments 3 Mins Read by Jack Godar SLU wrapped up one of the greatest regular seasons in program history, but the Billikens stumbled to the finish, going 3-3 in its last 6 games. Josh Schertz’s team tore through the A-10 with a 27-3 record and 15-3 record in conference play, led by an explosive and diverse offensive attack that led the A-10 in scoring at 87.3 points per game. SLU looked like a top ten team in January before their troubles in February, and have deep bench, with a 9-man rotation that wore teams out during the season and could be a big advantage in a tournament form. Robbie Avila leads the attack with 12.6 points per game and 4.1 assists per game, serving as the hub from which Trey Green, Ishan Sharma, and Brady Dunlap get open for a plethora of three pointers all over the court. Kellen Thames recovered from serious cramping issues last year to emerge as the most electric player in the A-10 as well, a human highlight reel of dunks, steals and energy plays. Tournament Path In the A-10 Tournament, SLU will face either George Washington or Fordham, and then potential opponents in the semi-finals if they win would include Dayton or George Mason, who each beat SLU this season. It could be a tough road, especially considering the Billikens’ recent form, ending their season with a 29-point loss to George Mason. After not being concerned with prior struggles, Josh Schertz’s alarms are up, and he has all week to make adjustments. Considering Schertz’s status as one of the best basketball minds in the entire game, the break between the regular season and A10 Tournament could be just what SLU needs to right the ship. Looming large over March, however, is Robbie Avila’s plantar fasciitis. It’s not typically an injury that goes away over a short period of time, and it clearly limited Avila against George Mason. He’s the hub of the offense and SLU is significantly better when he is in the game, so his health could determine SLU’s run not only in Pittsburgh, but in the NCAA Tournament if they make it. result.text: 2026 Atlantic 10 Tournament Previews: Saint Louis Billikens Close Menu News Tourney Central Blog Multimedia Photos Video Forums Teams Davidson Dayton Duquesne Fordham George Mason George Washington La Salle Loyola Rhode Island Richmond Saint Louis St. Bonaventure St. Joseph’s UMass VCU WBB Contributors Join Us Facebook X (Twitter) Instagram Facebook X (Twitter) Instagram News Tourney Central Blog Multimedia Photos Video Forums Teams Davidson Dayton Duquesne Fordham George Mason George Washington La Salle Loyola Rhode Island Richmond Saint Louis St. Bonaventure St. Joseph’s UMass VCU WBB Contributors Join Us You are at: Home » Blog » 2026 Atlantic 10 Tournament Previews: Saint Louis Billikens 2026 Atlantic 10 Tournament Previews: Saint Louis Billikens By Samuel Basel March 11, 2026 No Comments 3 Mins Read by Jack Godar SLU wrapped up one of the greatest regular seasons in program history, but the Billikens stumbled to the finish, going 3-3 in its last 6 games. Josh Schertz’s team tore through the A-10 with a 27-3 record and 15-3 record in conference play, led by an explosive and diverse offensive attack that led the A-10 in scoring at 87.3 points per game. SLU looked like a top ten team in January before their troubles in February, and have deep bench, with a 9-man rotation that wore teams out during the season and could be a big advantage in a tournament form. Robbie Avila leads the attack with 12.6 points per game and 4.1 assists per game, serving as the hub from which Trey Green, Ishan Sharma, and Brady Dunlap get open for a plethora of three pointers all over the court. Kellen Thames recovered from serious cramping issues last year to emerge as the most electric player in the A-10 as well, a human highlight reel of dunks, steals and energy plays. Tournament Path In the A-10 Tournament, SLU will face either George Washington or Fordham, and then potential opponents in the semi-finals if they win would include Dayton or George Mason, who each beat SLU this season. It could be a tough road, especially considering the Billikens’ recent form, ending their season with a 29-point loss to George Mason. After not being concerned with prior struggles, Josh Schertz’s alarms are up, and he has all week to make adjustments. Considering Schertz’s status as one of the best basketball minds in the entire game, the break between the regular season and A10 Tournament could be just what SLU needs to right the ship. Looming large over March, however, is Robbie Avila’s plantar fasciitis. It’s not typically an injury that goes away over a short period of time, and it clearly limited Avila against George Mason. He’s the hub of the offense and SLU is significantly better when he is in the game, so his health could determine SLU’s run not only in Pittsburgh, but in the NCAA Tournament if they make it.
